corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: If we call these classes in the training set “the positive classes,” then we want to be careful to avoid false positives on rubbish inputs–i.e., we do not want to classify a degenerate input as being something real.

top_candidates.sentence: For example shallow RBF networks with are only able to confidently predict that the positive class is present in the vicinity of µ.

Several machine learning models, including neural networks, consistently misclassify adversarial examples-inputs formed by applying small but intentionally worst-case perturbations to examples from the dataset, such that the perturbed input results in the model outputting an incorrect answer with high confidence.
Using this approach to provide examples for adversarial training, we reduce the test set error of a maxout network on the MNIST dataset.
1 INTRODUCTION made an intriguing discovery: several machine learning models, including state-of-the-art neural networks, are vulnerable to adversarial examples.
In many cases, a wide variety of models with different architectures trained on different subsets of the training data misclassify the same adversarial example.
This view enables us to design a fast method of generating adversarial examples that makes adversarial training practical.

Those most relevant to this paper include: • Box-constrained L-BFGS can reliably find adversarial examples. • On some datasets, such as ImageNet , the adversarial examples were so close to the original examples that the differences were indistinguishable to the human eye. • The same adversarial example is often misclassified by a variety of classifiers with different architectures or trained on different subsets of the training data. • Shallow softmax regression models are also vulnerable to adversarial examples. • Training on adversarial examples can regularize the model-however, this was not practical at the time due to the need for expensive constrained optimization in the inner loop.

Indeed, and have already begun the first steps toward designing models that resist adversarial perturbation, though no model has yet succesfully done so while maintaining state of the art accuracy on clean inputs.

Indeed, and have already begun the first steps toward designing models that resist adversarial perturbation, though no model has yet succesfully done so while maintaining state of the art accuracy on clean inputs.
We can thus view L 1 weight decay as being more "worst case" than adversarial training, because it fails to deactivate in the case of good margin.
Because L 1 weight decay overestimates the amount of damage an adversary can do, it is necessary to use a smaller L 1 weight decay coefficient than the associated with the precision of our features.

We can't expect a model with such low capacity to get the right answer at all points of space, but it does correctly respond by reducing its confidence considerably on points it does not "understand." RBF units are unfortunately not invariant to any significant transformations so they cannot generalize very well.
We can view linear units and RBF units as different points on a precision-recall tradeoff curve.
We can view linear units and RBF units as different points on a precision-recall tradeoff curve.
Linear units achieve high recall by responding to every input in a certain direction, but may have low precision due to responding too strongly in unfamiliar situations.
Linear units achieve high recall by responding to every input in a certain direction, but may have low precision due to responding too strongly in unfamiliar situations.
RBF units achieve high precision by responding only to a specific point in space, but in doing so sacrifice recall.
